coredata.dc:title: Microwave assisted synthesis of luminescent carbonaceous nanoparticles from silk fibroin for bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Hongzhi
Teng, Choon Peng
Huang, Donghong
Xu, Wanqing
Zheng, Chaohui
Chen, Yisong
Liu, Minghuan
Yang, Da-Peng
Lin, Ming
Li, Zibiao
Ye, Enyi
coredata.dc:description: Bombyx mori silk as a natural protein based biopolymer with high nitrogen content, is abundant and sustainable because of its mass product all over the world per year. In this study, we developed a facile and fast microwave-assisted synthesis of luminescent carbonaceous nanoparticles using Bombyx mori silk fibroin and silk solution as the precursors. As a result, the obtained carbonaceous nanoparticles exhibit a photoluminescence quantum yield of ~20%, high stability, low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility. Most importantly, we successfully demonstrated bioimaging using these luminescent carbonaceous nanoparticles with excitation dependent luminescence. In addition, the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method can be extended to convert other biomass into functional nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment
Luminescent carbonaceous nanoparticles
Excitation dependent luminescence
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbonaceous nanoparticles from degummed silk and silk solutions
Preparation and characterization of carbonaceous nanoparticles
Optical properties of the carbonaceous nanoparticles
In vitro cytotoxicity and cell bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the preparation of silk fibroin by degumming the raw Bombyx mori silk.

Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) imaging of the obtained carbonaceous nanoparticles using degummed silk (A, B) and silk solution (C,D) as the starting materials, (A) STEM images of carbonaceous nanoparticles at lower magnification and (B) at higher magnification, (C) STEM images of carbonaceous nanoparticles at lower magnification and (D) at higher magnification.
Heating profiles and in-situ pressure of the reactions of 15mL ~3% silk solution (upper panel) and mixture of 0.5g degummed silk with 15mL DI water (lower panel).

Characteristic optical spectra of CNPs prepared from degummed silk. (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and photoluminescence emission spectrum excited at 390nm. Inset: Photograph taken under room light (left) and under 365nm UV irradiation (right). (B) Photoluminescence emission spectra of CNPs measured under excitation from 300 to 532nm.

Characteristic optical spectra of CNPs prepared from silk solution. (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and photoluminescence emission spectrum excited at 450nm. Inset: Photograph taken under room light (left) and under 365nm UV irradiation (right). (B) Photoluminescence emission spectra of CNPs measured under excitation from 350 to 532nm.
Viability of CCD-112 CoN colon fibroblast cells incubated with CNPs prepared from silk solution at different concentrations.

Confocal microscopy images of CCD-112 CoN colon fibroblast cells, collected from the emission wavelength 430–490nm with excitation wavelength λex =405nm. (A–C) the corresponding bright-field images, luminescent images and overlapped images of the living cells; (D–F) the corresponding Z-stack bright-field images, luminescent images and overlapped images of the living cells.
Comparison of the emission peaks of CNPs prepared by degummed silk and silk solution respectively.

head.ce:title: Microwave assisted synthesis of luminescent carbonaceous nanoparticles from silk fibroin for bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Quanzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering & Materials Science, Quanzhou Normal University, Quanzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering & Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering, 2 Fusionopolis Way, Innovis, #8-03, Singapore 138634, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: D-P Yang, Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Quanzhou, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bombyx mori silk as a natural protein based biopolymer with high nitrogen content, is abundant and sustainable because of its mass product all over the world per year. In this study, we developed a facile and fast microwave-assisted synthesis of luminescent carbonaceous nanoparticles using Bombyx mori silk fibroin and silk solution as the precursors. As a result, the obtained carbonaceous nanoparticles exhibit a photoluminescence quantum yield of ~20%, high stability, low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility. Most importantly, we successfully demonstrated bioimaging using these luminescent carbonaceous nanoparticles with excitation dependent luminescence. In addition, the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method can be extended to convert other biomass into functional nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few decades, great progress has been made in the research of nanomaterials and nanotechnology [1–6]. Inorganic nanomaterials including metals [7–19], semiconductors [20–22], oxides [23–29], CNTs [30,31], graphene [32–34] etc. are of particular interest due to their chemical, catalytic, optical, electrical, magnetic, mechanical and other properties. A lot of researchers have been attracted to study photoluminescent nanomaterials, focusing on either semiconducting quantum dots or lanthanide based upconversion nanocrystals [35–44]. Until more recently, carbon based nanomaterials exhibiting luminescent properties attracted a lot of research efforts after the discovery of Xu et al. in 2004 [45]. In addition to luminescent properties, the luminescent carbon nanomaterials also possess properties of environmental friendly compositions, photochemical stability and aqueous solubility, making them promising for bioimaging, diagnosis, biosensing and drug delivery [46–49]. When compared with luminescent semiconducting quantum dots and lanthanide based upconversion nanocrystals, similar to aggregation-induced-emission (AIE) materials [50,51], the luminescent carbon based nanomaterials are superior in surface functionalities due to their intrinsic rich of carboxyl groups; they also possess distinct properties such as low photobleaching, low toxicity, no optical blinking, and excellent biocompatibility [52]. According to the documented reports, numerous synthetic methods have been developed to prepare luminescent carbon based nanomaterials, i.e. arc discharge [45], electrochemical synthesis [53], laser ablation [54], pyrolysis [55] and hydrothermal [56]. However, the quantum yields (QYs) of the result carbon nanomaterials were relatively low, which hinders their practical applications. In order to circumvent the problems, different strategies including surface passivation and inorganic ions doping [57]. It has been demonstrated that passivizing the luminescent carbon based nanomaterials with amino containing polymers such as polyetherimide (PEI) and amino-terminated polyethylene glycol can enhance their properties and expand their applications [58]. Despite this, the post treatment may be complicated and may bring in potential cytotoxicity, causing the luminescent carbon nanomaterials not suitable for bioimaging. Thus, there is still the need of developing a simple and effective method to prepare nitrogen-doped luminescent carbon nanomaterials.

There is a trend to prepare carbon based nanomaterials from natural materials especially biomass, based on the fact that biomass is cheap, nontoxic, rich in nitrogen and carbon, and easy to obtain [59–67]. As typical biomass material, cocoon silk is massively produced per year since Bombyx mori silkworm is cultivated all over the world. Bombyx mori silk is protein-based natural biopolymer consisting of fibroin as the core and sericin as the shell, with high nitrogen content of about 18%, making it an ideal precursor for preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon based nanomaterials [68]. Herein, we report a simple and fast microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment of Bombyx mori silk fibroin (or silk solution) to prepare aqueous dispersible, nitrogen-doped luminescent carbonaceous nanoparticles (CNPs) in water, without any digesting additives. The morphology of the obtained CNPs was characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The optical properties were studied using UV–vis spectrophotometer and fluorophotospectrometer, our results show that CNPs obtained from both silk fibroin and silk solution are highly luminescent with QY of around 20%. Viability test using Alarmablue assay confirms the low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of the obtained CNPs. More importantly, we successfully demonstrate that these highly luminescent CNPs can be used for live cell bioimaging.

Lithium Bromide (LiBr, 99%), Calcium Chloride (CaCl2, anhydrous 97%), absolute Ethanol (99%), Sodium Carbonate (Na2CO3, 99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were used without further purification. Cocoons of Bombyx mori were purchased from a silk farm.

In a typical process illustrated in Fig. 1 , the silk cocoons were first cut into pieces and the worms were disposed at the same time. Two liters of deionized water were added into a 3-liter glass beaker, covered with aluminum foil and were heated on a hotplate until boiling. Then 4.2g of sodium carbonate were added into the above boiling water and allowed to be completely dissolved. Add 5g of cocoon pieces into the boiling sodium carbonate solution and continue to boil for 30min. Finally the silk fibroin was removed with a spatula and cooled by rinsing with cold DI water. The cooled silk fibroin was rinsed with plenty of DI water for a least 3 times to completely remove sodium carbonate and sericin. The silk fibroin was squeezed to remove water content and spread on a piece of aluminum foil, and dry in a fume hood overnight.

As the native Bombyx mori silk is composed of two main components, with silk fibroin within and sericin without. When the native silk is boiled in sodium carbonate solution under the same condition, the removal of the sericin proteins is proportional to the boiling time. In order to maintain the consistency of the experimental results, the silk cocoons were degummed with the same protocol all the time. This is to ensure the same quality of silk fibroin from each batch of degumming.

Two methods were used to prepare silk solution. In the first method, 3g of degummed silk fibroin was packed tightly into a 30-mL glass vial, and 12mL LiBr solution (9.3M) was added on top. The vial was then put in an oil bath of 60°C for 4h, the silk fibroin was completely dissolved judging by its amber color and transparency. Then the silk-LiBr solution was transferred into a dialysis cassette (cut-off MW 3.5kD), and dialyzed against 2L DI water, the water was changed after 1h, 4h, that evening and the next morning, afternoon, night, the morning on the following day (i.e. 6–7 changes within 2days). The silk solution was collected and purified by centrifugation at 10000rpm at 4°C for 20min three times to remove any solid impurities. The silk solution was then stored at 4°C fridge for further usage.

In the second method, Ajisawa's method was applied [69]. In brief, 1.5g degummed silk fibroin was mixed with 10g of Ajisawa's reagent (CaCl2/Ethanol/H2O with molar ratio of 1:2:8), then the mixture was heated at 70°C for 3h under magnetic stirring and cooled to room temperature. The purification of the silk solution was conducted with the same way as the first method mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbonaceous nanoparticles from degummed silk and silk solutions
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment, 0.5g of degummed cocoon silk was mixed with 15mL DI water and loaded into a 30-mL reaction vessel. The vessel was then sealed with septa and placed into a microwave reactor (Monowave 300, Anton Parr). The mixture was quickly heated to 200°C in period of ~2min and the temperature was maintained for another 4h. The reaction was then cooled down with pressurized air flow. The carbonaceous nanoparticles were then collected by removing the undispersible materials by filtration with normal filter paper. The carbonaceous nanoparticles solution was dialyzed against DI water using a dialysis cassette with cut-off molecular weight of 3.5kD. And then the carbonaceous nanoparticles were further purified by filtration through 0.2μm PTFE membranes, and were kept in fridge for further usage.

When the silk solutions were used as the starting materials, mixture of degummed silk fibroin with 15mL water was replaced by 15mL silk solution while the other reaction procedures remained the same. The collection and purification of the obtained carbonaceous nanoparticles were also the same.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the carbonaceous nanoparticles were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co. Ltd., Japan). The luminescent spectra were measured using RF-5301PC fluorophotospectrometer (Shimadzu Co. Ltd., Japan). The size and morphology of the obtained carbonaceous nanoparticles were imaged on a FEI Titan TEM using Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy (STEM) mode. The luminescence imaging was carried out with a Leica TCS SP5 X confocal microscope with laser excitation of 405nm.

Viability test of carbonaceous nanoparticles was performed on CCD-112 CoN colon fibroblast cells by alamarblue assay. In a typical test, CCD-112 CoN colon fibroblast cells were seeded at a density of 2.0×104 cells/cm2 into black 96-well plate (Corning) and incubated overnight at 37.0°C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. On the following day, cells were incubated with fresh cell medium containing different concentrations of carbon dots in five replicates. After 24h incubation, the cells were washed with PBS and 100μL of 0.1mg/mL of alamarBlue assay (Life Technologies) was added to each well for evaluating the viability of the cells. The reducing environment of the viable cells converts the blue resazurin contents of alamarblue assay into highly fluorescent red resorufin, which is measured using 540nm excitation by a microplate reader (Tecan). The viability is calculated by the fluorescence emission at 595nm of the samples divided by that of control cells without particle treatment and presented as percentage viability of the control cells.

The quantum yield of the CNPs prepared from silk solution was measured with UV-1800 spectrometer and spectrofluorophotometer using 0.10M quinine sulfate H2SO4 solution as reference. Three different concentrations of CNPs solutions with absorbance between 0.01 and 0.1 were prepared, in order to minimize any possible re-absorption effects. The absorption and photoluminescence spectra of both CNPs samples and reference solution were measured. The quantum yield of the CNPs was calculated with the following equation:
Ф s
=
Ф r
m s
/
m r
ɳ s
2
/
ɳ r
2
where Ф is the quantum yield, m is the gradient by plotting integrated PL intensity against absorbance, ɳ is the refractive index of the solvent, s denotes sample and r denotes reference. In our study, the quantum yield for quinine sulfate H2SO4 solution is Ф
r
=0.54, the refractive index of quinine sulfate H2SO4 solution and CNPs in DI water are both η=1.33 [70]. The quantum yield of the CNPs obtained was calculated to be 20.5%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of carbonaceous nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) images (Fig. 2 ) showed that carbonaceous nanoparticles (CNPs) obtained from both degummed silk and silk solution have excellent dispersion without any aggregations. It was found that the CNPs obtained from silk solution are larger than those obtained from degummed silk.

As is reported, amino acids glycine (Gly), serine (Ser), tyrosin (Tyr) and alanine (Ala) are the main constituents of the silk fibroins, in repetitive sequence of Gly-Ala-Gly-Ala-Gly-[Ser-Gly-(Ala-Gly)n]8-Ser-Gly-Ala-Ala-Gly-Tyr with weak bonds. Due to the strong solvation effect and high reactivity of the water molecules at high temperature of 200°C, the degummed silk will be digested into fragments eventually amino acids. Which then become molecular precursors for carbonaceous nanomaterials. Similar to the conventional hydrothermal carbonization of carbohydrates, the protein fragments/amino acids undergoes dehydration and polymerization to obtain the CNPs. Thanks to the advance in the microwave reactors, the temperature and pressure of the reaction mixture can be monitored and recorded in-situ. As was shown in Fig. 3 , unlike the conventional hydrothermal treatment, the temperature of the reaction was swiftly elevated to 200°C within 2min for both degummed silk and silk solution. By comparing the pressure recordings, it was found that the pressure in the reaction using silk solution as the starting material is higher than that using degummed silk as starting material, indicating that the carbonization process is quicker in silk solution than in the degummed silk/water mixture. This result supports the proposed top-down first then bottom-up process for the formation of CNPs. More time is needed to break down the degummed silk into the fragments and amino acids for the formation of CNPs; thus silk solution would be ideal precursor for the formation of CNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the carbonaceous nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The CNPs prepared by the microwave assisted hydrothermal treatment show good absorption and emission properties. Contributed by the π-π⁎ transitions in the CNPs, their UV–vis spectrum showed a broad absorption in the UV region with two peaks at 275nm and 325nm (Fig. 4A). The CNPs obtained from degummed silk exhibit a strong photoluminescence emission peak centered at 460nm when excited at 390nm (Fig. 4A). When the aqueous CNPs dispersion was under irradiation of 365nm UV light, it exhibits blue-green color as shown in the inset in Fig. 4A. Similar to what has been reported on luminescent carbon dots, these CNPs shows excitation dependent emissions. As is shown in Fig. 4B, the emission peaks shift to longer wavelength as the excitation wavelength is increased. It is worth to note that the emission intensity increases with excitation wavelength increasing from 300 to 390nm, while the emission intensity decreases with excitation wavelength increasing from 390 to 532nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and cell bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: As is shown above, the CNPs prepared from silk solution exhibit better optical properties than that prepared from degummed silk. Thus it was chosen for demonstration of live cell bioimaging. Biocompatibility is an important factor for applications in bio-related areas. Before we use these CNPs for bioimaging, we tested their biocompatibility on CCD-112 CoN colon fibroblast cells using alamarBlue assay. Different concentrations of CNPs were tested; results shown in Fig. 6 indicate that these luminescent CNPs are highly biocompatible.

CCD-112 CoN colon fibroblast cells were seeded on 8-well plates at a density of 2.0×104 cellscm−2 and incubated overnight at 37.0°C. On the next day, 200μgmL−1 of CNPs were added to each well and incubated for 4h at 37.0°C for cell uptake. The cells were then washed thrice with PBS to remove the unassociated CNPs. The cells were then fixed using 4% paraformaldehyde (Sigma-Aldrich) in PBS for 10min at room temperature and rinsed with PBS twice. Triton X-100 with concentration of 0.2% was then used to permeabilize the cells for staining. After cell fixation and permeabilization, the cell nuclei were stained with propidium iodide for 20min and then rinsed with PBS. Imaging was performed using a confocal microscope system (Leica TCS SP5 X) equipped with a 40×dry objective. The images of 1024×1024pixels were processed using Leica application suite software. Fig. 7 shows the confocal images of CCD-112 CoN colon fibroblast cells impregnated with CNPs. From the image, we can clearly observe an obvious intracellular luminescence. When combined with bright-field image, it is found that the luminescence signals are mainly from the perinuclear area, suggesting excellent cell permeability of the CNPs into the living cells. This is double confirmed by the Z-stack images shown in Fig. 7D-F. It is also worth to note that under the confocal laser irradiation no blinking was observed and the photo-bleaching is very low, strongly suggesting these CNPs are of high stability. Thus these luminescent CNPs can be considered to have low cytotoxicity and to be suitable for both in vitro and in vivo bioimaging applications.

In summary, a facile, fast and green microwave-assisted synthetic method has been developed to prepare nitrogen-doped luminescent CNPs from Bombyx mori silk. The characterization of the optical property shows the excitation dependent luminescence of these carbonaceous nanoparticles, with quantum yields of about 20% and very good photo-stability. In addition, viability test indicates that these nitrogen-doped carbon dots are of low cytotoxicity. With their excellent luminescence and good biocompatibility, these nitrogen-doped CNPs can be used for bioimaging. We have successfully demonstrated imaging of living cells using these CNPs. More importantly, microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment of either degummed silk (top-down) or silk solution (bottom-up) leads to formation of highly luminescent CNPs in a quicker way, we do believe this method can be used for the preparation of functional carbonaceous nanomaterials from natural biomass with slight modifications.
